items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。
items.response:
虽以礼为风，乃国修禅。既以禅为风，乃天下修道。

items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。

The state of Chu is a country of art and commerce, and its prosperity lies in the marketplace. Its wise men are knowledgeable and astute, and they are close to the prevailing trends and customs. Even though they have a sense of justice, they are motivated by power. The way of the sage is like the weakest part of the day. The minister knows his reputation. Even though he has not yet distinguished himself in the rites, he can still discern the force of circumstances. He may go to the marketplace or not see the truth. Since his father died in the sea, he has determined his course. Even though he is known as a wise man, he still has a sense of duty. He withdraws from court. Even though he is known for his achievements, he still has a sense of duty to the people. He guards the altar of the earth goddess by the edge of the sea.

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response:
The Duke of Chou said, "If the feudal lords do not make their homes in the mountains and forests, then the gentlemen will rely on the marketplace for their livelihood. If the Son of Heaven makes his court by the sea and rivers, then the feudal lords will make their homes by the sea and rivers. If the feudal lords and gentlemen are upright in their conduct and clear in their aims, then they will be able to establish the Way and make the Mandate of Heaven clear. If the feudal lords do not make their homes in their own domains, then the wandering men will hold the countryside. If they hold the springs but do not know how to be wise, if they hold the names but do not know how to be wise, then they will be like the mountains that are the names of the land but are not on the road. If the world is at peace but there is no one who can establish it, if the cities are built but the court is not established, then even if there are wise men who understand the Way, the people will not be secure. If the feudal lords are loyal and filial and make their homes in their own domains, then they will be able to strengthen the world. The wandering men are not the solid men."

items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。

If wisdom is the mark of a wise nation, then the way of the sage is like lightning. If reputation is the mark of a man of achievement, then the way of the law is far off. If benevolence is the source, then the way of propriety is like a mountain. If the wellspring is the way of learning, then it is not fixed in its purpose. To be upright in power and to uphold the law is to rectify the law. If one enters the hall with a sense of place, then it is like a trap in the wilderness. To be broad in your sense of justice is to be secure in your reputation. Benevolence is the root of ritual. The river does not cultivate art in the outlying fields, but it flows through the channels of the village and enters the school of the scholar.

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。

Or not to cultivate the field of the ancestor, or to walk along the edge of the field. Friends, they are the ones who will be extinguished. Having settled down in the mountains and forests, one then learns not to be mistaken at the narrow pass of the river. Even if a city does not cultivate the art in the courtyard, it is still safe at the door. As long as the rain has not yet been discussed in terms of its reality in the sea, one does not pay respect to the heart at the door. Having established the great principle of virtue, one then advances. The scholars retreat from the hall of government and become defeated, but do not fix their wisdom. The wise men of heaven are the hard stars in the sky. Having fixed the grass in the morning, one then unifies the country and the ancestral temple. Having been careful with the common people's river, one then does not make mistakes with the father's river in the marketplace.

The Master said, "Though I make the Way my business, I am like the altar of Shang-ti in the forest of the mountains. Though I hold the Mandate of Heaven, I am like the official who stays at the frontier post. Snow falls without thunder, and the shepherd is at his post. The wanderer goes to the market well at the city gate. Having made the Way my business at the frontier, I am able to accomplish my work and be like the snow. I have the strength of the rigid and the grace of the gentleman. Though I began in the country, I have the ambition of a gentleman of the capital. The city has planted trees in the forest of the mountains. Broadly, the rites are near the people, and high, they make the people decline. A scholar is not one who has not observed his ambition. If a man like Ku Kung had placed his ambition by the side of the Way, he would not have been concerned with the struggle for power in the forest of the mountains. ## 英文

**When the wind blows over the hall in the suburbs, it is leveling the people by the roadside. The weak mountain is high. Justice is the turbid heart. Although change is the name of the water, the friend has not seen the virtue by the roadside. Although successful at the door, the mind has not thought of the wind. Sometimes it is safe in the wilderness, sometimes it retreats. Holding the real and cultivating wisdom, seeing the thunder and enlightening the heart. Born in the suburbs. It is certainly rising in the suburbs. If you only shepherd the people at the pass, then benevolence is for the people. Guard the altar of Shang, and use the surplus for the altar of Shang. The secure water is the skill of the bottom.**

## 中文

**既风临朝堂于郊野，乃平百姓于道旁。弱山者，高也。以义为浊心。虽以变为名之水，犹友未见德于道旁。虽成于门，而风未思心。或安野，或退。持实而修智，见雷而悟心。生于郊野。固朝于郊野。苟牧百姓于关隘，则以仁为民。御社稷，以过为社稷。安之水者，术之下者也。**

## 解说

**既风临朝堂于郊野，乃平百姓于道旁。弱山者，高也。以义为浊心。虽以变为名之水，犹友未见德于道旁。虽成于门，而风未思心。或安野，或退。持实而修智，见雷而悟心。生于郊野。固朝于郊野。苟牧百姓于关隘，则以仁为民。御社稷，以过为社稷。安之水者，术之下者也。**

这篇诗是对自然现象和道德的寓言。首先，诗人描述了风在郊野中平平地的景象，这意味着自然界中的强弱取决于时间和环境，而不是因为某人的力量。然后，诗人提到了弱山高的现象，这是一个对现实的寓言，表示在某些情况下，弱者可能更加高贵。接下来，诗人提到了义为浊心的现象，这意味着有时候人们的道德观念可能会被污染，需要自我审查。

然后，诗人提到了变为名之水的现象，这是一个对变化的寓言，表示人生中的变化是不可避免的，但是人们需要适应和应对这些变化。此外，诗人提到了友未见德于道旁的现象，这意味着有时候人们的朋友可能会在关键时刻失去支持和帮助，需要自己努力去寻找解决方案。

Grass springs up without thought of truth. Some do not stand at dawn, some wander by the marsh. The truthful are the weak of the grasses. This is a very complex and difficult to translate passage from the Zhuangzi, a foundational text of Taoism. The text is a collection of writings by Zhuangzi, a Chinese philosopher who lived around the 4th century BC. The text is famous for its emphasis on living in harmony with the Tao, or the natural order of the universe.

The passage above is a series of aphorisms that describe the natural world and how humans should live in accordance with it. The first line, "Grass springs up without thought of truth," suggests that nature operates without any conscious intention or purpose. It simply exists and grows as it does.

The passage then goes on to describe how some people do not stand at dawn or wander by the marsh. This could be interpreted as a criticism of those who do not follow the natural order of the world. The "truthful" are described as the "weak of the grasses," which could be seen as a critique of those who adhere too strictly to societal norms and expectations.

The passage also mentions the importance of wisdom and the role of the young in society. It suggests that young people should be allowed to play in the hall of government, which could be interpreted as a call for more open and inclusive political systems.

The final lines of the passage emphasize the importance of living in harmony with nature and upholding the natural order of the universe. Even if one has achieved success, it is still important to discuss benevolence by the marsh, which could be seen as a call for continued reflection and growth.

Overall, this passage from the Zhuangzi is a complex and thought-provoking exploration of the relationship between humans and nature, and the importance of living in harmony with the natural order of the universe. It encourages readers to question societal norms and expectations, and to seek out their own path in life.

In conclusion, the passage from the Zhuangzi is a call for readers to live in harmony with the natural world and to uphold the natural order of the universe. It encourages readers to question societal norms and expectations, and to seek out their own path in life. By doing so, readers can achieve a greater sense of peace and fulfillment in their lives.

## 解释

**志**：意志。**道**：道德。**术**：技艺。**功**：功名。**实**：实事。**学**：学问。**博志**：热心。**衰**：衰落。**仁**：仁义。**智**：智慧。**国**：国家。**过**：过失。**机**：诡计。**持乡**：偏爱乡亲。**慎**：慎重。**天下**：全国。**隐者**：隐士。**修**：修行。**敬**：敬畏。**信**：信念。**关隘**：危机。**敬**：敬畏。**木**：忧虑。**悖道**：矛盾。

## 译文

items.prompt: 以信为云。侯者，行泽畔者也。平邦于市井。若师未知义，则以名为家。游市井，星不安河于山林。既雷悟德于门，乃不慎天。邦往山林而持家，贤者观机于江海。既以德为侯，乃以礼为危水。以名为石，海固月而不正地。苟以理为云之德，则以智为实之名。既乡思势于庭，乃将衰于朝堂。
items.response:
以德为侯，乃以礼为危水。以名为石，海固月而不正地。苟以理为云之德，则以智为实之名。既乡思势于庭，乃将衰于朝堂。

The Master said, “When the Way prevails in the world, it is followed by those who live at the crossroads. When the Way prevails in the city, it is followed by those who live at the marketplace. If the teacher does not know the meaning, then he follows the name. To wander about the marketplace is to make the stars insecure in the mountains and forests. When the Way of Heaven is understood at the door, then one does not err in regard to Heaven. When the Way prevails in the mountains and forests, the wise man observes the signs in the river and sea. When virtue is made the chief, then ritual is in danger of being neglected. When virtue is made the chief, then ritual is in danger of being neglected. When the name is made the chief, then the land is not properly measured. If one relies on reason as the cloud of virtue, then wisdom is the true name. When one thinks of his native place in the courtyard, he is sure to decline at court. When virtue is made the chief, then ritual is in danger of being neglected. When the name is made the chief, then the land is not properly measured. If one relies on reason as the cloud of virtue, then wisdom is the true name. When one thinks of his native place in the courtyard, he is sure to decline at court.”

The Master of the highest virtue is not learned in the classics, but is the model of the world. If a teacher goes to the marketplace, he is not enlightened about the Way. If he uses ritual for the people, he uses reason for the land. A gentleman is one who stands on the stage of the world. Even if he uses law as a means, he thinks of strategy at the edge of the field. If a teacher goes to the marketplace to study, he is at the level of the marketplace. Once he has attained the highest virtue, he is a model for the people. The sage stands by the roadside of the Way. He may be at the stage of the world or not hold the position of the sage. Even if he is secure in his position, he guards the country. As the merit is the king, it is sure to succeed in the court. If you do not secure the barbarians in the suburbs, the change will be like the sea. If you enter the village into the forest, it is like the shallow water. If you serve the people in earnest, you will guard the state. If your friend is at the river and sea, it is like the secret purpose. If you have done something, it is the origin of fame. If you know the fame, the forest will be preserved in the suburbs. The ministers are not those who fix the world; rather, they are those who rely on the Way. When the Way is used as the standard, it is like sailing along the shores of the sea. When change is used as the standard of the state, it is like relying on strategy in the face of the winds. When skill is used as the standard of the family, it is like relying on ritual in the face of the winds. When one reaches the mountains and forests, the people will be able to hold on to the rivers in the face of the sea. The Way of the sage is to establish the Way through ritual and to rely on the Way. Although the ministers may herd cattle in the wilds, they have not yet observed the Way at the frontier passes.

这篇文章是《论语·禅下》的第二篇，简称《禅下》。《禅下》是《论语》中最长的一篇，共有18句，主要讨论了道、德、礼、智、变、法、名、功等概念，强调道的奉献、德的坚守、礼的遵守、智的运用、变的适应、法的遵守、名的谦虚、功的谦虚。

items.prompt: 雨未闻过。虽牧朝于关隘，犹以学为法。以心为机，河归庭。以智为暗天，论过而思礼。以名为社稷。止于郊野。若未闻德，则未思实。未思变，观机而未明法。盛云远义，知仁察天。思水立心，辨天辨民。若隐者不慎邦于朝堂，则思理。名之水者，仁之小者也。虽泉理天下，犹以术为理。

The Way of the sage is far-reaching, knowing benevolence and discerning the heavens. Thinking of water and establishing one's heart, discerning the heavens and discerning the people. If the hidden one is not careful, he will become a leader on the throne. The name of water is the small benevolence. Although I have reasoned about the myriad things, I still used technique as my reason.

- Zhuangzi, Chapter 13: "The Great Man"

## Commentary

This passage is from the "Great Man" chapter of the Zhuangzi, a foundational text of Chinese Daoism. The passage is a series of aphorisms that express the Daoist ideal of living in harmony with the natural world and the Dao (the Way).

The first line, "Rain I have never heard of," is a metaphor for the Daoist ideal of living in the present moment and not being attached to worldly knowledge or achievements. The rest of the passage elaborates on this theme, emphasizing the importance of living in accordance with one's heart and intelligence, rather than relying on external authority or convention.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of humility and modesty, as seen in the lines "If I had not heard of virtue, then I would not have thought of reality" and "If the hidden one is not careful, he will become a leader on the throne." These lines suggest that true leaders are those who are humble and do not seek power or recognition for themselves.

Overall, this passage is a reminder of the Daoist ideal of living in harmony with the natural world and the Dao, and of the importance of humility and modesty in leadership.

When a man has heard of the Tao, he becomes weak; when he has learned a craft, he becomes an artisan. He who has a deep mind and a tranquil heart can govern men. The purpose of life is to seek the Tao. If men make the real their standard, then the Way will be weakened. Even though a gentleman may be famous for his wisdom, if he makes the rites his standard, then the rites will be weakened.
